name: Huelga Utrera - Cueva del Agua - Pontones Trail (PR-A 195)
name_original: Charco del humo y cueva del agua desde Pontones - circular por el P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas
description: Hike the difficult 10.5-mile Huelga Utrera - Cueva del Agua - Pontones Trail (PR-A 195) through diverse landscapes and karstic caves.
region_intro: Hiking around Santiago-Pontones offers exploration within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, characterized by a varied topography. The region features an orographic labyrinth of valleys, gorges, and mountains, with elevations ranging from 600m to 1993m. Extensive forests, including colossal Laricio pine trees, dominate the landscape, alongside significant hydrological features like the source of the Segura River and numerous waterfalls. Karstic formations are also present, contributing to unique geological features. Best hiking trails around Santiago-Pontones The most popular hiking route is Puntal de los Canteros y de la Pililla - circular by the P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas, a 9.1 miles (14.7 km) trail that takes 4 hours 58 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers panoramic views of the natural park. Another top favourite among local hikers is Huelga Utrera - Cueva del Agua - Pontones Trail (PR-A 195), a difficult 10.5 miles (16.8 km) path. This trail allows for exploration of fascinating karstic caves. Local hikers also love the Water Cave from Huelga Utrera - Cazorla, Segura and Las Villas S. N. P., a 5.0 miles (8.0 km) trail leading through the natural park, often completed in about 2 hours 31 minutes. Hiking around Santiago-Pontones is defined by extensive forests, significant rivers, and diverse karstic formations. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate paths to more challenging mountain climbs. The routes in Santiago-Pontones are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 150 reviews. More than 1300 hikers have used komoot to explore Santiago-Pontones's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Santiago-Pontones?
A: Santiago-Pontones offers a diverse network of over 40 hiking trails. These routes cater to various skill levels, from easy strolls to challenging mountain climbs, all set within the stunning Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Santiago-Pontones?
A: The region experiences a humid continental climate with high mountain influences. While winters can be cold and snowy, the summers are generally cool and pleasant, making it an inviting destination for hiking enthusiasts. Spring and autumn offer particularly mild temperatures and vibrant natural scenery.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in Santiago-Pontones?
A: Yes, there are several options suitable for families. The region has made efforts to enhance accessibility on some trails, such as the "Las Pozas del Zumeta" trail, which features improved infrastructure and signage. With 8 easy routes available, you can find paths that are less strenuous and enjoyable for all ages.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Santiago-Pontones?
A: The Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park generally welcomes dogs, but it's always recommended to keep them on a leash to protect the local wildlife and ensure their safety. Always check specific trail regulations before heading out, as some areas might have restrictions.

Q: Are there any hikes that feature waterfalls or significant water features?
A: Absolutely. Santiago-Pontones is hydrologically rich, hosting the source of the Segura River and numerous waterfalls. You can explore dramatic cascades like the Cascada de Linarejos, often lauded as the most beautiful in the Natural Park, or the impressive Salto de los Órganos, a 170-meter cascade. The Borosa River also offers picturesque trails.

Q: Where can I find trails that explore the unique karstic formations and caves?
A: The geological makeup of Santiago-Pontones features striking karstic formations, grottoes, and caves. A notable route for exploring these is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e982927564/huelga-utrera-cueva-del-agua-pontones-trail-pr-a-195" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Huelga Utrera - Cueva del Agua - Pontones Trail (PR-A 195)&lt;/a&gt;. You can also visit highlights like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5335467" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva del Agua&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6050845" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuevas del Engarbo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in the area?
A: Yes, Santiago-Pontones offers several circular routes. One popular option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e987647222/puntal-de-los-canteros-y-de-la-pililla-circular-by-the-p-n-de-las-sierras-de-cazorla-segura-y-las-villas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puntal de los Canteros y de la Pililla - circular by the P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 9.1-mile trail offering panoramic views. Another is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983587792/the-cottages-of-madrono-circular-through-the-p-n-of-the-sierras-de-cazorla-segura-y-las-villas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The cottages of Madroño - circular through the P. N. of the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of wildlife can I expect to see while hiking?
A: The natural park is a haven for biodiversity. You might spot various mammals, birds, reptiles, amphibians, and a remarkable number of butterfly species. Keep an eye out for deer, wild boar, and various raptors that inhabit the extensive forests and rugged terrain.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: For experienced hikers seeking a challenge, Santiago-Pontones has 10 difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983578848/puntal-de-la-misa-to-las-gorgollitas-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puntal de la Misa to Las Gorgollitas Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 11.2-mile route with significant elevation changes, offering rewarding views and a true test of endurance.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Santiago-Pontones?
A: The trails in Santiago-Pontones are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 150 reviews. Hikers often praise the region's diverse landscapes, from extensive forests with colossal Laricio pine trees to dramatic river valleys and panoramic viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1752638" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de las Palomas Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any specific viewpoints or panoramic spots accessible by trail?
A: Yes, several peaks and passes offer sweeping panoramic views of the natural park. Trails around Puntal de los Canteros y de la Pililla provide excellent vistas. You can also explore highlights such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1752638" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de las Palomas Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2305421" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Despiernacaballos Pass&lt;/a&gt; for breathtaking scenery.
tips: Beautiful waterfall that when hitting the rocks forms a thick mist. This fact makes the place known as the Charco del Humo. One more singularity of this beautiful corner of Segura Pontones where all this land is a hymn to the wonders of nature.

At the entrance to Poyotello –a small and singular village belonging to the municipality of Santiago Pontones– a path starts that continuously descends and will take us to the water cave.
From the Cueva del Agua we can continue the path, which always runs parallel to the Segura riverbed until it ends at the village of Huelga Utrera.

faqs.question: Where is the trailhead for the Huelga Utrera - Cueva del Agua - Pontones Trail (PR-A 195) and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The trail is linear and can be started from either Huelga Utrera or Pontones. Many hikers prefer to begin in Huelga Utrera. You'll find sections of the route on a road near Huelga Utrera and a narrow track before Pontones, suggesting parking might be available in or near these villages.

faqs.question: Is the PR-A 195 trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the trail is located within the Parque Natural de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas, dogs are generally permitted. However, to protect local wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all, it's essential to keep your dog on a leash at all times.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the diverse flora and the climate of Andalusia, the best times to hike this trail are typically spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October). During these seasons, temperatures are milder, and the natural scenery, including the Corsican pine forests and various deciduous trees, is particularly vibrant or beautifully colored.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for the Huelga Utrera - Cueva del Agua - Pontones Trail?
faqs.answer: No, there is no general entrance fee or permit required to hike the Huelga Utrera - Cueva del Agua - Pontones Trail (PR-A 195). It is freely accessible as part of the Parque Natural de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas. However, always be mindful of park regulations regarding camping, fires, and waste.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Huelga Utrera - Cueva del Agua - Pontones Trail?
faqs.answer: The full linear route is approximately 16.8 km (10.4 miles) and is rated as difficult. It typically takes around 5 to 6 hours to complete, depending on your pace, fitness level, and how long you spend enjoying the scenic viewpoints and highlights like the Charco del Humo and Cueva del Agua.

faqs.question: Is the PR-A 195 suitable for beginner hikers?
faqs.answer: The Huelga Utrera - Cueva del Agua - Pontones Trail (PR-A 195) is generally rated as difficult due to its length and elevation gain. While some sections might be moderate, the overall route requires a good level of physical fitness and proper hiking experience. It may not be ideal for absolute beginners.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain and scenery can I expect on the route?
faqs.answer: You'll experience a rich variety of landscapes. The trail follows the Segura River, passing through Corsican pine forests, vegetable patches, and areas with large walnut trees. As the valley narrows, you'll encounter dramatic vertical limestone walls, numerous caves, and steep slopes covered in holm oaks. The path largely uses old bridle paths, with some forest tracks and a small section on a road.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Huelga Utrera - Cueva del Agua - Pontones Trail?
faqs.answer: Key highlights include the impressive Charco del Humo, a powerful waterfall with transparent pools, and the Cueva del Agua (Water Cave), a significant karstic formation offering unforgettable views over the Segura Canyon. The trail also features diverse flora and fauna, including potential sightings of golden eagles.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of the Huelga Utrera - Cueva del Agua - Pontones Trail (PR-A 195) overlap with other routes. You'll find it intersects with parts of the Andalucía - North Route (Província de Jaén) and the Transandalus, as well as several segments of the Pontones-Poyotello trail.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a hike on this trail?
faqs.answer: Given its difficulty and length, you should bring plenty of water, snacks, appropriate hiking footwear with good grip (due to potentially slippery sections), sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and layers of clothing suitable for changing mountain weather. A map or GPS device is also recommended, as is a first-aid kit.

faqs.question: Is there an option for a circular route instead of a linear one?
faqs.answer: The Huelga Utrera - Cueva del Agua - Pontones Trail (PR-A 195) is primarily described as a linear route. While you can hike it in both directions, there isn't a direct circular option for the entire PR-A 195 mentioned in the trail descriptions. You would need to arrange transport back to your starting point if you complete the full linear trail.
